region_of_origin: China (Central and Northern regions, including Hubei)
food_subgroup: Prepared vegetable dishes / Chinese cold dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lotus root food product entry; no specific entry for the prepared cold salad form exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Liangban Oupian. Closest defensible anchor used under prepared lotus root vegetable category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared dish. Omitted to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Lotus Root Salad is approximately 120 g, equivalent to a small side-dish bowl of thinly sliced, marinated lotus root.

nutrient_source.title: Lotus root, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169998); supplemented with estimated dressing contribution for prepared salad form
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw lotus root used as the primary nutritional anchor. Macronutrient and micronutrient values were adapted to reflect the prepared cold-salad form: blanching causes minor nutrient losses (estimated 5–10% for water-soluble vitamins); the dressing (rice vinegar, soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, garlic) contributes additional sodium (~120–150 mg/100 g increment), fat (~1.5–2 g/100 g from sesame oil), and sugar (~0.5–1 g/100 g from added sugar). Final per-100 g values represent the composite prepared dish. Input record values cross-checked against USDA data and found to be within plausible range; retained with adaptation notation.

gi_source.title: Lotus root — GI estimated by analogy to root vegetables with high dietary fibre; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Liangban Oupian (cold lotus root salad) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy to lotus root and high-fibre root vegetables documented in Atkinson et al. (2008). The presence of vinegar (acetic acid) in the dressing is documented to further attenuate postprandial glucose response (Johnston et al., Eur J Clin Nutr 2010). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 11.4) / 100 ≈ 4.0 (carbohydrate per 120 g serving = 9.5 × 1.2 = 11.4 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce dressing (320 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced soy sauce, as the 320 mg sodium per 100 g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets; the dish's potassium content (350 mg/100 g) partially offsets this risk by supporting vascular tone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and high dietary fibre (3.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lotus Root Salad is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its low GI of approximately 35, high dietary fibre, and vinegar-based dressing collectively moderate postprandial blood glucose response, and a standard 120 g serving delivers a glycemic load of only ~4.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high fibre, and antioxidant polyphenols from lotus root
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is broadly cardioprotective due to its low saturated fat content (~0.5 g/100 g), meaningful dietary fibre (3.5 g/100 g), and the antioxidant polyphenols in lotus root; however, patients on sodium-restricted cardiac diets should moderate intake due to the soy sauce component.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols and antioxidants in lotus root
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lotus root contains quercetin and other polyphenols with documented anti-inflammatory properties, making this dish a reasonable dietary inclusion for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lotus Root Salad (凉拌藕片, Liángbàn Ǒupiàn) is a traditional Chinese cold dish originating from Central and Northern China, particularly associated with Hubei cuisine. It is prepared by briefly blanching thin, decorative slices of fresh lotus root to preserve their characteristic crisp, crunchy texture and pale ivory colour, then immediately chilling them before tossing in a savory-tart vinaigrette made from rice vinegar, light soy sauce, minced garlic, a small amount of sugar, and sesame oil. Red chilies or chili oil are commonly added for a mild heat accent. The dish is served cold as a starter or side dish, providing a refreshing, palate-cleansing contrast to richer main courses. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 75 kcal, 2.2 g protein, 3.8 g fat, 9.5 g carbohydrate, and 3.5 g dietary fibre. It is a notable source of potassium (350 mg), vitamin C (25 mg), and iron (1.2 mg). The sodium content is moderate at 320 mg/100 g, primarily from soy sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), and a standard 120 g serving carries a glycemic load of only ~4, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegan, vegetarian, and dairy-free, though it contains soy and sesame allergens. It is widely consumed as an appetizer, snack, or cooling side dish at lunch and dinner.
common_user_queries: What is Liang Ban Ou Pian?
Is lotus root salad good for diabetics?
How is 凉拌藕片 made?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for the prepared cold salad form of lotus root was found in any primary approved source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw lotus root data (FDC ID 169998) with estimated adjustments for blanching losses and dressing contribution. Input record values were cross-checked and found plausible. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE ADAPTATION: Per-100 g values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw lotus root (FDC ID 169998) with adjustments for blanching and the vinaigrette dressing (soy sauce, sesame oil, rice vinegar, sugar, garlic). No approved-source entry for the exact prepared dish was available. 2. ZERO-VALUE FIELDS: vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — lotus root contains negligible provitamin A carotenoids (confirmed naturally zero per USDA FoodData Central). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; lotus root and the dressing ingredients contain no vitamin D. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero; this is a vegan dish. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero; no hydrogenated fats are used. 3. NEGATIVE SENTINEL: water_g is set to -1 because moisture content for the prepared cold-mixed dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for this composite preparation. 4. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on high-fibre root vegetables and the documented glucose-attenuating effect of acetic acid (vinegar) in the dressing; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. SODIUM CAUTION: At 320 mg sodium per 100 g, individuals with hypertension or on sodium-restricted diets should moderate portion size or reduce soy sauce. 6. ALLERGENS: Contains soy (soy sauce) and sesame (sesame oil); not suitable for individuals with soy or sesame allergies. 7. DUPLICATE PORTION in input: The input record contained two identical portion entries; deduplicated to one standard serving of 120 g. 8. MULTILINGUAL SCOPE: This is a Chinese dish; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and left null. G6 multilingual gate is satisfied by the presence of Chinese simplified and traditional script forms.
